{
  "name": "mlai-au",
  "private": true,
  "type": "module",
  "packageManager": "bun@1.2.15",
  "engines": {
    "bun": ">=1.2.15",
    "node": ">=18.0.0"
  },
  "scripts": {
    "check:article-internal-links": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/check-article-internal-links.ts",
    "generate:sitemap": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/generate-sitemap.ts",
    "prebuild": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/generate-sitemap.ts",
    "build": "NODE_OPTIONS='--max-old-space-size=8192' react-router build",
    "postbuild": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/submit-indexnow.ts",
    "cf-typegen": "bunx --yes wrangler types",
    "deploy": "bun run build && bunx --yes wrangler deploy",
    "dev": "node scripts/dev-local.mjs",
    "dev:node": "node scripts/dev-local.mjs",
    "preview": "bun run build && vite preview",
    "typegen": "bun run cf-typegen 1>/dev/null && react-router typegen",
    "typecheck": "react-router typegen && tsc -b && bun run check:article-internal-links"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "@ffmpeg/ffmpeg": "^0.12.15",
    "@ffmpeg/util": "^0.12.2",
    "@headlessui/react": "^2.2.9",
    "@heroicons/react": "^2.2.0",
    "@radix-ui/react-slot": "^1.2.4",
    "@rowanmanning/feed-parser": "^2.1.1",
    "@tailwindcss/typography": "^0.5.19",
    "axios": "^1.13.2",
    "class-variance-authority": "^0.7.1",
    "clsx": "^2.1.1",
    "date-fns": "^4.1.0",
    "isbot": "^5.1.32",

runContext.design_guide:
# EXISTING ARTIFACT MLAI — Brand & Design Guide — All Surfaces Version: 1.1 (React Router + article system adaptation) Scope: Branding, tokens, theming, component usage + defaults, accessibility, and article-content implementation rules. Audience: Engineers, designers, AI agents. Goal: Build UI/content that is visually consistent, inclusive, and build-safe in this repository without guessing. Applies to: `app/articles/content/**`, shared article components in `app/components/articles/**`, and related UI surfaces. --- ## 1) Brand identity ### 1.1 Palette (tokens) MLAI uses dark neutrals + vivid accents (teal/orange/purple/yellow). In this codebase, both CSS variables and hardcoded accent hexes are used. | Token | Hex | Role | |---|---:|---| | `--brand` | `#00E6C2` | Brand accent surface / bullets / emphasis | | `--brand-ink` | `#007A66` | Brand text/link colour on light surfaces | | `--accent` | `#111827` | Primary dark text | | `--soft` | `#E0FFFA` | Soft brand-tinted backgrounds | | `--highlight` | `#F59E0B` | Highlight accent (limited emphasis) | | Accent Orange | `#ff3d00` | MLAI bold card/callout surface | | Accent Purple | `#4b1bd1` | MLAI bold card/callout surface | | Accent Yellow | `#ffe900` | MLAI high-contrast card/callout surface | #### Guardrails (WCAG AA) - On light backgrounds, use `text-gray-900`/`text-gray-800`/`text-[--brand-ink]`. - Avoid white text on bright teal unless contrast is verified. - On orange/purple surfaces, use `text-white`. - On yellow surfaces, use `text-black`. - Links on light backgrounds: `text-[--brand-ink] underline-offset-4 hover:underline`. --- ### 1.2 Typography, spacing, shape - Tailwind utilities are the default styling system. - Typography plugin is enabled (`@tailwindcss/typography`). - Rounded styles in active use: - `rounded-lg`, `rounded-2xl`, `rounded-3xl`, `rounded-[28px]`, `rounded-[32px]` - Prefer generous spacing in article modules (`my-8`, `my-12`, `p-6`, `p-8`). --- ### 1.3 Motion & focus - Keep transitions subtle (`transition-colors`, `duration-200`). - Focus styles should remain visible (`focus:ring-*`, `focus-visible:ring-*`). - Do not remove focus outlines. --- ### 1.4 Tone & integrity - AU English. - Practical, clear, no hype/guarantees. - No unverifiable claims of outcomes. - Keep CTAs generic (e.g. **“Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}”**). - Do not add medical disclaimers unless explicitly requested in source template/content requirements. --- ## 2) Theming & tokens for code ### 2.1 Framework and styling reality (authoritative) - Framework: **React Router 7** - Rendering: **React 19** - Language: **TypeScript (`.tsx`)** - Styling: **Tailwind CSS v4** - Imports: **alias-first with `~/...`** - Internal links: `Link` from `react-router` + `to` prop ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' ``` ### 2.2 Tailwind usage in this repo - Tailwind config is minimal with typography plugin. - Design tokens are often consumed through CSS vars in classes: - `bg-[--soft]` - `border-[--brand]` - `text-[--brand-ink]` - Brutalist tokens are also used in some UI components (`--brutalist-*`). --- ## 3) Surface rules (article-focused) - **Use `bg-transparent` for content wrappers** (never introduce `bg-white` wrappers in article content files). - Borders are commonly neutral: `border border-gray-300` / `border-gray-400`. - Strong visual modules can use MLAI accents (orange/purple/yellow) via existing components. --- ## 4) Global component rules (applies to article content) ### Architecture constraints (must follow) 1. **Do NOT import/use `ArticleLayout` in content files** under `app/articles/content/**`. 2. Route handler/layout already renders title/breadcrumb/author/meta; **do not duplicate top header blocks** in content bodies. 3. Prefer design-system article components over raw custom UI blocks. 4. Do not use non-existent variants/props. 5. Do not use placeholder image services (e.g. placehold). Use real URLs or placeholders like ``. ### Component priority rules - Persona/audience cards → `AudienceGrid` - Quotes/callouts → `QuoteBlock` - Images → `ArticleImageBlock` - FAQ sections → `ArticleFAQ` - References → `ArticleReferences` - Resources list → `ArticleResourceList` --- ## 5) Components — one-shot recipes (repo-accurate) ### 5.1 AudienceGrid (for “Who is this for?”) ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' ``` Valid variants: `'orange' | 'purple' | 'yellow'` only. --- ### 5.2 QuoteBlock (for callouts/quotes) ```tsx import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' Keep your first implementation narrow and measurable. ``` Valid variants: `'purple' | 'orange'` only. --- ### 5.3 ArticleImageBlock (for all article images) ```tsx import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' ``` Do not use raw `` in article content when this component is available. --- ### 5.4 ArticleStepList ```tsx import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' ``` Use `accent="teal"` or `accent="indigo"` in new content. Avoid `brand` in generated templates. --- ### 5.5 ArticleFAQ ```tsx import { ArticleFAQ } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' ``` --- ### 5.6 ArticleReferences ```tsx import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' ``` --- ### 5.7 ArticleResourceList ```tsx import { ArticleResourceList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceList' ``` --- ### 5.8 Internal navigation links (React Router) ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' Browse all articles ``` --- ## 6) Accessibility checklist (copy into every PR) - [ ] Colour contrast meets WCAG AA. - [ ] Focus visible on interactive elements. - [ ] Semantic heading order is valid. - [ ] Icon-only controls include `aria-label`. - [ ] Links are distinguishable and keyboard accessible. - [ ] Images have meaningful `alt` (or decorative handling where appropriate). --- ## 7) Copy/content rules for article generation - Keep tone practical and specific. - Avoid guarantees or inflated claims. - Use concise sections, scannable subheadings, and actionable bullets. - Keep CTA labels generic (e.g. “Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}”). - No domain-specific “service chips” or injected promo taxonomies unless source content explicitly requires them. --- ## 8) Patterns & recipes (ready for AI) ### 8.1 Article content file skeleton (`app/articles/content/.../*.tsx`) ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' export default function ArticleContent() { return ( <>

Start with one high-impact workflow and a clear success metric.

Set a 30-day checkpoint before scaling. ) } ``` --- ### 8.2 TOC placeholder pattern ```tsx import ArticleTocPlaceholder from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' ``` --- ### 8.3 Footer navigation pattern ```tsx import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' ``` --- ## 9) File locations (conventions) - Article content files: `app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx` - Article components: `app/components/articles/*` - Shared components: `app/components/*` - Use alias imports (`~/...`) consistently. --- ## 10) Registry/SEO/path integrity rules - `seo_config` path must match registry slug path exactly. - Keep slug/file-path consistency across: - content file path - article registry entry - route slug resolution --- ## 11) “Do / Don’t” quick list ### Do - Use React Router `Link` from `react-router`. - Use article components (`AudienceGrid`, `QuoteBlock`, `ArticleImageBlock`) instead of ad-hoc UI. - Keep wrappers `bg-transparent`. - Keep CTA copy generic and reusable. - Keep imports alias-first (`~/...`). ### Don’t - Don’t import `ArticleLayout` in content modules. - Don’t duplicate hero/title/breadcrumb sections in content body. - Don’t use invalid variants (`blue`, unsupported `brand` usage in templates). - Don’t use placeholder image services. - Don’t invent component props not defined in codebase. --- ## Appendix A — Framework guardrails - Do not use Next.js APIs (`next/link`, `next/image`, `getStaticProps`, `metadata`, `use client`). - Do not use Vue/Svelte syntax. - Use `.tsx` + React Router patterns only. --- ## Appendix B — Build-safety checks - Verify all imports resolve (`~/components/articles/...` etc.). - Ensure no missing/renamed component imports. - Keep content components declarative and type-safe. - Prefer existing component APIs exactly as implemented.
runContext.company_context:
MLAI is a Melbourne-based not-for-profit community for Australia’s AI, machine learning and startup ecosystem. It runs events, hackathons, educational resources, Slack-based community programs and founder tools with a stated mission to help create 1,000 Australian startups by 2030.

Problem solved: Australian AI builders and founders need practical ways to learn, meet collaborators, access events and maintain startup momentum.

Target audience: Australian AI and machine learning builders, startup founders, students, professionals, sponsors and ecosystem partners.

# EXISTING ARTIFACT MLAI — Brand & Design Guide — All Surfaces Version: 1.1 (React Router + article system adaptation) Scope: Branding, tokens, theming, component usage + defaults, accessibility, and article-content implementation rules. Audience: Engineers, designers, AI agents. Goal: Build UI/content that is visually consistent, inclusive, and build-safe in this repository without guessing. Applies to: `app/articles/content/**`, shared article components in `app/components/articles/**`, and related UI surfaces. --- ## 1) Brand identity ### 1.1 Palette (tokens) MLAI uses dark neutrals + vivid accents (teal/orange/purple/yellow). In this codebase, both CSS variables and hardcoded accent hexes are used. | Token | Hex | Role | |---|---:|---| | `--brand` | `#00E6C2` | Brand accent surface / bullets / emphasis | | `--brand-ink` | `#007A66` | Brand text/link colour on light surfaces | | `--accent` | `#111827` | Primary dark text | | `--soft` | `#E0FFFA` | Soft brand-tinted backgrounds | | `--highlight` | `#F59E0B` | Highlight accent (limited emphasis) | | Accent Orange | `#ff3d00` | MLAI bold card/callout surface | | Accent Purple | `#4b1bd1` | MLAI bold card/callout surface | | Accent Yellow | `#ffe900` | MLAI high-contrast card/callout surface | #### Guardrails (WCAG AA) - On light backgrounds, use `text-gray-900`/`text-gray-800`/`text-[--brand-ink]`. - Avoid white text on bright teal unless contrast is verified. - On orange/purple surfaces, use `text-white`. - On yellow surfaces, use `text-black`. - Links on light backgrounds: `text-[--brand-ink] underline-offset-4 hover:underline`. --- ### 1.2 Typography, spacing, shape - Tailwind utilities are the default styling system. - Typography plugin is enabled (`@tailwindcss/typography`). - Rounded styles in active use: - `rounded-lg`, `rounded-2xl`, `rounded-3xl`, `rounded-[28px]`, `rounded-[32px]` - Prefer generous spacing in article modules (`my-8`, `my-12`, `p-6`, `p-8`). --- ### 1.3 Motion & focus - Keep transitions subtle (`transition-colors`, `duration-200`). - Focus styles should remain visible (`focus:ring-*`, `focus-visible:ring-*`). - Do not remove focus outlines. --- ### 1.4 Tone & integrity - AU English. - Practical, clear, no hype/guarantees. - No unverifiable claims of outcomes. - Keep CTAs generic (e.g. **“Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}”**). - Do not add medical disclaimers unless explicitly requested in source template/content requirements. --- ## 2) Theming & tokens for code ### 2.1 Framework and styling reality (authoritative) - Framework: **React Router 7** - Rendering: **React 19** - Language: **TypeScript (`.tsx`)** - Styling: **Tailwind CSS v4** - Imports: **alias-first with `~/...`** - Internal links: `Link` from `react-router` + `to` prop ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' ``` ### 2.2 Tailwind usage in this repo - Tailwind config is minimal with typography plugin. - Design tokens are often consumed through CSS vars in classes: - `bg-[--soft]` - `border-[--brand]` - `text-[--brand-ink]` - Brutalist tokens are also used in some UI components (`--brutalist-*`). --- ## 3) Surface rules (article-focused) - **Use `bg-transparent` for content wrappers** (never introduce `bg-white` wrappers in article content files). - Borders are commonly neutral: `border border-gray-300` / `border-gray-400`. - Strong visual modules can use MLAI accents (orange/purple/yellow) via existing components. --- ## 4) Global component rules (applies to article content) ### Architecture constraints (must follow) 1. **Do NOT import/use `ArticleLayout` in content files** under `app/articles/content/**`. 2. Route handler/layout already renders title/breadcrumb/author/meta; **do not duplicate top header blocks** in content bodies. 3. Prefer design-system article components over raw custom UI blocks. 4. Do not use non-existent variants/props. 5. Do not use placeholder image services (e.g. placehold). Use real URLs or placeholders like ``. ### Component priority rules - Persona/audience cards → `AudienceGrid` - Quotes/callouts → `QuoteBlock` - Images → `ArticleImageBlock` - FAQ sections → `ArticleFAQ` - References → `ArticleReferences` - Resources list → `ArticleResourceList` --- ## 5) Components — one-shot recipes (repo-accurate) ### 5.1 AudienceGrid (for “Who is this for?”) ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' ``` Valid variants: `'orange' | 'purple' | 'yellow'` only. --- ### 5.2 QuoteBlock (for callouts/quotes) ```tsx import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' Keep your first implementation narrow and measurable. ``` Valid variants: `'purple' | 'orange'` only. --- ### 5.3 ArticleImageBlock (for all article images) ```tsx import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' ``` Do not use raw `` in article content when this component is available. --- ### 5.4 ArticleStepList ```tsx import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' ``` Use `accent="teal"` or `accent="indigo"` in new content. Avoid `brand` in generated templates. --- ### 5.5 ArticleFAQ ```tsx import { ArticleFAQ } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' ``` --- ### 5.6 ArticleReferences ```tsx import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' ``` --- ### 5.7 ArticleResourceList ```tsx import { ArticleResourceList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceList' ``` --- ### 5.8 Internal navigation links (React Router) ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' Browse all articles ``` --- ## 6) Accessibility checklist (copy into every PR) - [ ] Colour contrast meets WCAG AA. - [ ] Focus visible on interactive elements. - [ ] Semantic heading order is valid. - [ ] Icon-only controls include `aria-label`. - [ ] Links are distinguishable and keyboard accessible. - [ ] Images have meaningful `alt` (or decorative handling where appropriate). --- ## 7) Copy/content rules for article generation - Keep tone practical and specific. - Avoid guarantees or inflated claims. - Use concise sections, scannable subheadings, and actionable bullets. - Keep CTA labels generic (e.g. “Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}”). - No domain-specific “service chips” or injected promo taxonomies unless source content explicitly requires them. --- ## 8) Patterns & recipes (ready for AI) ### 8.1 Article content file skeleton (`app/articles/content/.../*.tsx`) ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' export default function ArticleContent() { return ( <>

Start with one high-impact workflow and a clear success metric.

Set a 30-day checkpoint before scaling. ) } ``` --- ### 8.2 TOC placeholder pattern ```tsx import ArticleTocPlaceholder from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' ``` --- ### 8.3 Footer navigation pattern ```tsx import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' ``` --- ## 9) File locations (conventions) - Article content files: `app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx` - Article components: `app/components/articles/*` - Shared components: `app/components/*` - Use alias imports (`~/...`) consistently. --- ## 10) Registry/SEO/path integrity rules - `seo_config` path must match registry slug path exactly. - Keep slug/file-path consistency across: - content file path - article registry entry - route slug resolution --- ## 11) “Do / Don’t” quick list ### Do - Use React Router `Link` from `react-router`. - Use article components (`AudienceGrid`, `QuoteBlock`, `ArticleImageBlock`) instead of ad-hoc UI. - Keep wrappers `bg-transparent`. - Keep CTA copy generic and reusable. - Keep imports alias-first (`~/...`). ### Don’t - Don’t import `ArticleLayout` in content modules. - Don’t duplicate hero/title/breadcrumb sections in content body. - Don’t use invalid variants (`blue`, unsupported `brand` usage in templates). - Don’t use placeholder image services. - Don’t invent component props not defined in codebase. --- ## Appendix A — Framework guardrails - Do not use Next.js APIs (`next/link`, `next/image`, `getStaticProps`, `metadata`, `use client`). - Do not use Vue/Svelte syntax. - Use `.tsx` + React Router patterns only. --- ## Appendix B — Build-safety checks - Verify all imports resolve (`~/components/articles/...` etc.). - Ensure no missing/renamed component imports. - Keep content components declarative and type-safe. - Prefer existing component APIs exactly as implemented.
org_config_snapshot.resource_prompt:
# EXISTING ARTIFACT ```markdown MLAI -- One-Shot Article Content Generation Spec (React Router TSX) -- v2026-04-26 Purpose. Hand this document directly to an AI agent (as a "system" / "developer" prompt) so the agent can generate brand-consistent MLAI article content modules in one shot for this repository. MLAI context. MLAI is a not-for-profit community organisation helping Australians learn about startups and artificial intelligence. Content should be practical, inclusive, and factual. Avoid hype and guarantees. Output format: TypeScript React component file (`.tsx`) for article body content. Framework: React Router 7 + React 19 + Tailwind. Import style: alias-first with `~/...` paths. --- ## 0) Quick-start contract for the agent Task: Given article inputs (slug/category/topic/body sections/FAQ/references/CTA text), generate a production-ready article content TSX file that compiles **without manual edits**. Framework constraints (required): - Use `react-router` APIs (e.g., `Link` with `to` prop). - Do **not** use Next.js APIs (`next/link`, `next/image`, `use client`, metadata exports, etc.). - Do **not** use Vue/Svelte syntax. - Use Tailwind utility classes consistent with this repo. Architecture constraints (required): - **NO `ArticleLayout` in content files**. Route handler wraps content already. - Content component must return `<>...` or `
...
`. - If using wrapper div, use `bg-transparent` (never `bg-white`). - Use design system components when available: - Persona cards → `AudienceGrid` (`variant`: `orange | purple | yellow` only) - Quotes/callouts → `QuoteBlock` (`variant`: `purple | orange` only) - Images → `ArticleImageBlock` (no raw `` blocks for article body sections) - Never use `placehold.co` URLs. Use `` or empty string placeholders. - Ensure `seo_config` path matches registry slug path exactly. Deliverable: - `app/articles/content/{{CATEGORY}}/{{SLUG}}.tsx` (If requested, also provide companion updates for registry/SEO entries with matching slug/path.) --- ## 1) Inputs you will receive Supply at minimum: - `title`: string - `slug`: string - `category`: string (folder under `app/articles/content/`) - `summary`: string - `sections`: ordered list of body sections - `audienceCards` (optional): for "Who is this for?" - `quoteBlocks` (optional) - `images` (optional) - `faqItems` (optional) - `references` (optional) - `cta` (optional, generic wording only) Use placeholders exactly as provided (e.g., `{{TOPIC}}`). --- ## 2) Framework, file, and styling configuration - Language: TypeScript (`.tsx`) - Component pattern: `export default function ...() { ... }` - Routing library: `react-router` - Styling: Tailwind classes already used in repo - Typography plugin enabled (`@tailwindcss/typography`) - Internal imports should prefer `~/...` aliases Common class patterns in this codebase: - Layout/spacing: `my-8`, `my-12`, `mt-8`, `rounded-[28px]`, `rounded-3xl`, `p-6`, `sm:p-8` - Text colors: `text-gray-900`, `text-gray-700`, `text-gray-600`, `text-white` - Borders/surfaces: `border border-gray-300`, `border-gray-400`, `bg-transparent` - Prose-safe wrappers for custom UI blocks: `not-prose` --- ## 3) Import rules (use real project paths) Use only imports that exist in this codebase. Typical imports: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' ``` Do not invent components/props. --- ## 4) Article content structure (no duplicate page header) Important: layout/hero/breadcrumb/title are handled upstream. So inside content file: - Start directly with body content (`h2`, `p`, lists, blocks). - Do **not** render duplicate title/breadcrumb/author header. - Keep heading hierarchy valid (`h2` for top-level sections, `h3` nested). Recommended section flow: 1. Intro paragraph(s) 2. Main sections (`h2`) 3. Optional persona block (`AudienceGrid`) 4. Optional quote block (`QuoteBlock`) 5. Optional image block (`ArticleImageBlock`) 6. FAQ (`ArticleFAQ`) 7. Generic CTA 8. Footer nav (`ArticleFooterNav`) --- ## 5) Design system component usage (strict) ### 5.1 Audience cards Use `AudienceGrid` for "Who is this for?" sections: ```tsx ``` Allowed variants only: `orange | purple | yellow`. ### 5.2 Quotes/callouts Use `QuoteBlock` (not ad-hoc bordered divs): ```tsx Keep your first AI implementation narrow, measurable, and reviewable. ``` Allowed variants only: `purple | orange`. ### 5.3 Images Use `ArticleImageBlock`: ```tsx ``` No placeholder services (e.g., no `placehold.co`). --- ## 6) Links, references, and navigation - Internal links: `Link` from `react-router` with `to`. - External links: `...`. - Use authoritative references when included. - Footer navigation can use: ```tsx ``` --- ## 7) CTA rules - Keep CTA copy generic and reusable. - ✅ Example: `Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}` - ❌ Do not use product-specific or service-specific names unless explicitly provided. Example with existing component: ```tsx ``` --- ## 8) Content quality and tone rules - Australian context where relevant. - Practical, factual, no guarantees. - No medical/legal-style warning blocks unless explicitly requested. - No service chips/tags sections. - Keep paragraphs concise and scannable. - Prefer concrete steps and examples. --- ## 9) Build-safety rules (one-shot accuracy) - File must compile with strict TypeScript. - Use valid, existing imports only. - Do not add unsupported props to components. - Keep variant strings within known unions. - Avoid fragile breadcrumb/icon patterns in content files. - Keep slug/path naming consistent across file path and SEO/registry references. --- ## 10) Brand QA checklist (must pass) - Uses repository design components for cards/callouts/images. - No duplicate hero/title section in content body. - Wrapper uses `bg-transparent` (never `bg-white`). - Tailwind classes align with existing style language. - CTA text remains generic with placeholders preserved. - No invented components. --- ## 11) Technical QA checklist (must pass) - React Router imports only (`react-router`). - No Next.js/Vue/Svelte APIs. - `.tsx` syntax valid. - All JSX tags closed and typed. - Internal links use `to`, external links use `href`. - No broken alias paths (`~/...`). --- ## 12) Critical do/don't summary (hard guardrails) DO - Generate `app/articles/content/{{CATEGORY}}/{{SLUG}}.tsx`. - Export a default function component. - Use `AudienceGrid`, `QuoteBlock`, `ArticleImageBlock` where applicable. - Use only allowed variants. - Preserve placeholders like `{{TOPIC}}`. - Keep content body-only (no layout wrapper). NEVER - Import or render `ArticleLayout` in content files. - Use `bg-white` wrapper backgrounds. - Use unsupported variants (`blue`, `brand` for these DS blocks). - Use `placehold.co`. - Invent imports, components, or props. - Add duplicate page title/breadcrumb header inside content. --- ## 13) Suggested content primitives (repo-aligned) - Section heading: `

...

` - Body copy: `

...

` - Persona block: `AudienceGrid` - Quote/callout: `QuoteBlock` - Image: `ArticleImageBlock` - FAQ: `ArticleFAQ` with `ArticleFAQItem[]` - CTA: `ArticleCompanyCTA` - Footer nav: `ArticleFooterNav` --- ## 14) Example scaffold (copy/paste starter) ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' const faqItems: ArticleFAQItem[] = [ { question: 'What is the first step?', answer: 'Define one clear outcome for {{TOPIC}}.' }, ] export default function ArticleContent() { return (

This guide explains practical ways to approach {{TOPIC}}.

Who is this for?

Key insight

Start narrow, track results, and iterate.
) } ``` --- ## 15) How to use this prompt 1. Parse inputs (`{{TOPIC}}`, slug, category, sections, FAQ, CTA). 2. Generate a single `.tsx` article content module at: - `app/articles/content/{{CATEGORY}}/{{SLUG}}.tsx` 3. Use existing repository components and exact import paths. 4. Keep output body-only (no `ArticleLayout` / duplicate hero). 5. Validate TypeScript + React Router compatibility. 6. Ensure slug/path consistency for any SEO/registry references requested. End of master spec (React Router TSX, MLAI codebase-aligned). ```
org_config_snapshot.company_context:
MLAI is a Melbourne-based not-for-profit community for Australia’s AI, machine learning and startup ecosystem. It runs events, hackathons, educational resources, Slack-based community programs and founder tools with a stated mission to help create 1,000 Australian startups by 2030.

Problem solved: Australian AI builders and founders need practical ways to learn, meet collaborators, access events and maintain startup momentum.

Target audience: Australian AI and machine learning builders, startup founders, students, professionals, sponsors and ecosystem partners.

tech_stack.framework_prompt_context: DETECTED FRAMEWORK: React Router RENDERING LIBRARY: react 19.2.1 ROUTING: react-router-7 (import from 'react-router', links use 'to' prop) STYLING: tailwind LANGUAGE: TypeScript COMPONENT FILE EXTENSION: .tsx BUILD TOOL: vite CRITICAL RULES FOR CODE GENERATION: - Do NOT use next/link, next/image, next/dynamic — these are Next.js-only packages - Do NOT use 'use client' or 'use server' directives — these are Next.js App Router only - Do NOT use getStaticProps, getServerSideProps, generateStaticParams — Next.js only - Do NOT use export const metadata — Next.js App Router only - Do NOT use next/font — Next.js only - Do NOT use Vue SFC (.vue files), Composition API, or Options API — not a Vue project - Do NOT use Svelte syntax (.svelte files), $: reactivity, or Svelte stores USE THESE PATTERNS INSTEAD: - For links: import from 'react-router' and use the 'to' prop - For images: use standard HTML tags - For styling: use plain CSS or the project's detected styling approach

pillars.name: AI Community Events and Meetups in Australia
pillars.description: This pillar focuses on discovering, choosing, and getting value from AI meetups, workshops, lightning talks, and community events across Australia. It directly supports MLAI's events programming for people who want practical learning, relevant peers, and a clearer entry point into the local AI community.
pillars.content_angle: A practical, community-first guide to finding real AI connections and hands-on learning through Australian events rather than generic networking.

pillars.name: AI Hackathons and Real-World Challenge Programs
pillars.description: This pillar covers hackathons, challenge-based programs, team formation, prototyping, and applied AI problem-solving. It aligns closely with MLAI's hackathons and event programs, helping builders, students, founders, and domain experts turn ideas into tangible projects.
pillars.content_angle: Show how hackathons can be practical pathways to collaboration, prototypes, and startup momentum, not just short-term coding competitions.

pillars.name: Practical AI Learning and Getting Started
pillars.description: This pillar helps beginners and early-stage practitioners learn AI and machine learning through accessible, community-based pathways. It supports MLAI's educational content, workshops, and events by answering common learning questions with practical next steps and applied examples.
pillars.content_angle: Lower the barrier to entry by pairing plain-English education with hands-on community participation instead of theory-heavy content alone.

pillars.name: AI Founder Support, Pitching and Investor Readiness
pillars.description: This pillar addresses how early-stage AI founders can build momentum, sharpen their pitch, create better updates, and start investor relationship-building earlier. It maps to MLAI's founder-oriented tools, practical guides, and community programs that help entrepreneurs move from idea to credible signal.
pillars.content_angle: Focus on founder communication and investor readiness as practical habits supported by community, feedback, and early visibility.
seed_keywords: how to pitch an ai startup

pillars.name: Cross-Disciplinary AI Collaboration
pillars.description: This pillar explores how technical builders, clinicians, researchers, and other domain experts can work together on real-world AI challenges. It reflects MLAI's community and hackathon model, where useful outcomes depend on better collaboration between technical and non-technical participants.
pillars.content_angle: Bridge the gap between builders and domain experts with practical guidance for team formation, problem definition, and applied outcomes.
seed_keywords: technical and non technical collaboration ai

pillars.name: Community Participation and Contributor Recognition
pillars.description: This pillar explains how members can contribute meaningfully inside a community, build credibility, and earn recognition through participation. It directly connects to MLAI's Slack community and Roo Points system, giving contributors a clearer path to involvement, visibility, and useful perks.
pillars.content_angle: Position contribution as a practical way to build relationships and credibility in an AI community, without framing it as a guaranteed pathway to jobs or compensation.
seed_keywords: how to contribute to a tech community

pillar_strategy.rationale: These pillars were chosen because they map directly to MLAI's strongest audience needs and core offerings: events, hackathons, practical learning, founder support, cross-disciplinary collaboration, and community participation. Together they cover the main discovery journeys of learners, builders, founders, contributors, and domain experts while staying grounded in MLAI's role as a practical, community-first home base for applied AI in Australia.

{
  "name": "mlai-au",
  "private": true,
  "type": "module",
  "packageManager": "bun@1.2.15",
  "engines": {
    "bun": ">=1.2.15",
    "node": ">=18.0.0"
  },
  "scripts": {
    "check:article-internal-links": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/check-article-internal-links.ts",
    "generate:sitemap": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/generate-sitemap.ts",
    "prebuild": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/generate-sitemap.ts",
    "build": "NODE_OPTIONS='--max-old-space-size=8192' react-router build",
    "postbuild": "bunx --yes tsx scripts/submit-indexnow.ts",
    "cf-typegen": "bunx --yes wrangler types",
    "deploy": "bun run build && bunx --yes wrangler deploy",
    "dev": "node scripts/dev-local.mjs",
    "dev:node": "node scripts/dev-local.mjs",
    "preview": "bun run build && vite preview",
    "typegen": "bun run cf-typegen 1>/dev/null && react-router typegen",
    "typecheck": "react-router typegen && tsc -b && bun run check:article-internal-links"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "@ffmpeg/ffmpeg": "^0.12.15",
    "@ffmpeg/util": "^0.12.2",
    "@headlessui/react": "^2.2.9",
    "@heroicons/react": "^2.2.0",
    "@radix-ui/react-slot": "^1.2.4",
    "@rowanmanning/feed-parser": "^2.1.1",
    "@tailwindcss/typography": "^0.5.19",
    "axios": "^1.13.2",
    "class-variance-authority": "^0.7.1",
    "clsx": "^2.1.1",
    "date-fns": "^4.1.0",
    "isbot": "^5.1.32",

spec.title: What Is a Technology Business Incubator?
spec.meta_description: What is a technology business incubator? Founder guide

summary_items.label: What is a business incubator?
summary_items.description: A business incubator is a program or organisation that helps early-stage ventures develop their business. A technology business incubator focuses on startups where modern technology is the main source of innovation.

summary_items.label: What is the purpose of a business incubator?
summary_items.description: The purpose is to improve a startup’s chance of survival and growth. Incubators do this through structure, mentors, resources, business support and access to useful networks.

summary_items.label: What are the three types of incubators?
summary_items.description: Common incubator types include industry-specific, university-affiliated and independent incubators. The article also covers virtual incubators, which support distributed teams through online programs and resources.

summary_question_candidates.question: What is the purpose of a business incubator?
What are the three types of incubators?
How does a tech incubator work?

faq_items.question: How does a tech incubator work?
faq_items.answer: A tech incubator usually selects ventures, provides resources and mentorship, tracks progress, and helps founders move toward market readiness, fundraising, graduation or another suitable next step.

faq_items.question: What services can a technology business incubator provide?
faq_items.answer: Services may include shared workspace, labs, meeting rooms, internet access, mentoring, workshops, business support, networking, investor introductions, grant pathways or seed funding connections, depending on the program.

faq_items.question: Is an incubator the same as an accelerator?
faq_items.answer: No. Incubators often support earlier-stage ventures over months or years, while accelerators are commonly shorter and more intensive. Exact models vary by program.

faq_items.question: How should founders choose a technology business incubator?
faq_items.answer: Founders should compare stage fit, technology focus, location, mentor network, program length, cost, equity expectations and access to relevant customers, partners, investors or funding pathways.

sections.heading: Why technology business incubators matter to early-stage founders
paragraphs: A technology business incubator, or TBI, supports startup companies and individual entrepreneurs that use modern technology as the main source of innovation. For early-stage founders, this matters because a technical idea, research result or prototype is not yet a business. Founders still need to test the idea, shape a useful product, understand customers and build a plan for growth.

Incubators are designed as structured support environments, not just desks or office space. They can offer mentorship, training, shared facilities, business support, networks and, in some cases, pathways to funding.

sections.heading: What is a technology business incubator?
paragraphs: A technology business incubator, often shortened to TBI, is an organisation or program that helps startup companies and individual entrepreneurs build businesses where modern technology is the main source of innovation. It is a type of business incubator, but its focus is narrower than general business support. It is built for founders working on technology-led products, services or ventures.

A TBI can support founders through training, workshops, mentoring, shared facilities, business services, networking and brokering connections. Some incubators may also help with financing, investor introductions, seed funding or grant applications, though this varies by program. The common goal is to reduce early barriers and give founders a more structured path from idea to viable business.

The value of a technology business incubator is not just desk space. Physical space, labs, meeting rooms or internet access can help, but the stronger benefit is usually the mix of structure, expertise, community and access. For an AI or machine learning founder in Australia, that might mean finding people who understand the technical problem, meeting potential collaborators, learning how to explain the business case, and staying connected to a startup community while the idea is still forming.

callout.title: Plain-English definition
callout.body: A technology business incubator helps technology-led founders turn an early idea into a stronger startup by giving them practical support, guidance, networks and sometimes access to funding.

sections.heading: How a technology incubator works from entry to graduation
paragraphs: A technology business incubator usually starts with fit. Founders research programs that match their industry, location, stage and needs. They then compare the services on offer, such as mentoring, workspace, workshops, networking and possible funding access. Many programs use an application and selection process, so founders may need to prepare a pitch deck, business plan, financial information or a clear explanation of the venture.

After acceptance, the incubator becomes a structured support environment rather than a one-off event. A startup may go through orientation, meet mentors, attend workshops, use shared facilities and join networking sessions. This support can last longer than an accelerator program, with some incubators working with startups for months or even years depending on the startup’s needs and the incubator’s policy.

subsections.heading: 1. Selection and fit
paragraphs: The entry phase is about choosing the right incubator and making a credible case to join. Founders look for alignment with their sector, location, program length, costs and support model. A good fit matters because incubators differ in focus, from industry-specific programs to university, independent and virtual models.

subsections.heading: 2. Incubation support
paragraphs: Once inside, the startup receives practical support to reduce early operating pressure and improve the chance of progress. This may include mentoring, training, office or lab space, meeting rooms, internet access, networking opportunities and business support services. Not every incubator offers funding, but some help founders connect with investors, grants or seed funding pathways.

subsections.heading: 3. Graduation or next-step readiness
paragraphs: Graduation is the point where the startup is ready to operate with more independence or move to another form of support. By then, the team may have clearer goals, stronger networks, better evidence of progress and a plan for the next stage. For some companies, that next step may be fundraising, market entry, a later-stage program or continued growth outside the incubator.

sections.heading: What services can founders expect inside an incubator?
paragraphs: Inside a technology business incubator, founders can usually expect a mix of practical infrastructure, advisory support and structured learning. The aim is to reduce early friction for startup teams. Instead of solving every operational problem alone, founders can use shared resources while they test ideas, build early products and prepare for market entry.

The exact offer varies by incubator. Some focus on physical space and facilities, while others are virtual or sector-specific. For AI and technology founders, the useful question is not only whether an incubator has desks. It is whether the program can improve access to mentors, peers, partners, customers, investors or funding pathways that match the company’s stage.

subsections.heading: Infrastructure and operating support
paragraphs: Many incubators provide shared office space, meeting rooms, internet access and basic facilities. Some may also provide laboratories, manufacturing space or other specialised facilities where that fits the startups they support.

subsections.heading: Mentors, training and networks
paragraphs: Incubators commonly add mentorship, workshops and business support around the workspace. Mentors and advisors can help founders think through product strategy, business models, market entry and operating decisions. Training may cover capability gaps such as legal, accounting, marketing, finance and other business basics.

Networks are also a core part of the value. Incubators may connect founders with other entrepreneurs, industry professionals, possible partners, clients, investors and grant or funding programs. Not every incubator offers funding directly, so founders should check whether the program provides seed funding, loan programs, grant application help or introductions to relevant investors.

sections.heading: Types of technology business incubators and when they fit
paragraphs: Not every technology business incubator is built for the same founder. For an AI or deep technology team, this kind of industry-specific fit can matter because the mentors, facilities, partners and investor introductions are more likely to understand the market you are entering.

University-affiliated incubators often suit ventures that grow from academic research, student projects or university-linked innovation. Independent incubators may support a wider mix of startups across industries. Virtual incubators can be useful for distributed teams because they provide support and resources online rather than requiring every founder to work from the same site.

Look at the incubator's industry focus, location, program length, mentor network, cost, equity expectations and access to funding pathways. Also check whether the program offers the kind of help you need now, such as mentoring, workshops, office space, networking opportunities, seed funding connections or grant application support.

sections.heading: Decide whether an incubator is your next best move
paragraphs: A technology business incubator is a good next move when you need more than a desk. It is a program or organisation that helps early-stage ventures develop their business through structure, mentors, resources, business support and access to useful networks. Its purpose is to improve a startup’s chance of survival and growth by helping founders work through early operational, financial and market challenges.

Before you apply, compare incubators by stage fit, technology focus, mentor quality, network strength, funding access, program length and any cost or equity terms. Common types include industry-specific, university-affiliated, independent and virtual incubators. A tech incubator usually works by selecting ventures, giving them resources and mentorship, tracking progress, and helping them move toward market readiness or graduation.

cta.title: Build with the right startup support
cta.body: Explore MLAI’s Australian AI community resources for practical learning, founder support, events and collaboration opportunities.

  { id: 1, question: "How does a tech incubator work?", answer: "A tech incubator usually selects ventures, provides resources and mentorship, tracks progress, and helps founders move toward market readiness, fundraising, graduation or another suitable next step." },
  { id: 2, question: "What services can a technology business incubator provide?", answer: "Services may include shared workspace, labs, meeting rooms, internet access, mentoring, workshops, business support, networking, investor introductions, grant pathways or seed funding connections, depending on the program." },
  { id: 3, question: "Is an incubator the same as an accelerator?", answer: "No. Incubators often support earlier-stage ventures over months or years, while accelerators are commonly shorter and more intensive. Exact models vary by program." },
  { id: 4, question: "How should founders choose a technology business incubator?", answer: "Founders should compare stage fit, technology focus, location, mentor network, program length, cost, equity expectations and access to relevant customers, partners, investors or funding pathways." },
]

    { label: "What is the purpose of a business incubator?", description: "The purpose is to improve a startup\u2019s chance of survival and growth. Incubators do this through structure, mentors, resources, business support and access to useful networks." },

  ],
}

    document.body.appendChild(script)
    return () => {
      script.remove()
    }
  }, [])
  return null
}

export default function ArticleContent() {
  const authorDetails = {
    name: AUTHOR,
    role: AUTHOR_ROLE,
    bio: AUTHOR_BIO,
    avatarUrl: AUTHOR_AVATAR,
  }

  return (
    <>
      {faqStructuredData ? (
        <script type="application/ld+json" dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: faqStructuredData }} />
      ) : null}
      <ContentFactoryInspectorBridge />
      <ArticleHeroHeader
        breadcrumbs={[
          { label: 'Home', href: '/', icon: Home },
          { label: 'Articles', href: "/articles" },
          { label: TOPIC, current: true },
        ]}
        title={TOPIC}
        titleHighlight={TOPIC}
        headerBgColor="cyan"
        summary={summaryHighlights}
        heroImage={HERO_IMAGE}
        heroImageAlt={HERO_IMAGE_ALT}
      />

      <ArticleTocPlaceholder className="bg-transparent" />

      <div className="prose prose-lg prose-slate max-w-none bg-transparent">
        <div id="section-01" data-cf-component-id={"section:section-01"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"Why technology business incubators matter to early-stage founders"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-01"}>
        <p><strong>{TOPIC}</strong> — {"A technology business incubator, or TBI, supports startup companies and individual entrepreneurs that use modern technology as the main source of innovation. For early-stage founders, this matters because a technical idea, research result or prototype is not yet a business. Founders still need to test the idea, shape a useful product, understand customers and build a plan for growth."}</p>
        <p>{"Incubators are designed as structured support environments, not just desks or office space. They can offer mentorship, training, shared facilities, business support, networks and, in some cases, pathways to funding."}</p>
        <div data-cf-component-id={"image:section-01"} data-cf-component-type={"image"} data-cf-component-label={"Hero image"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-01"}>
        <ArticleImageBlock
          src={HERO_IMAGE}
          alt={HERO_IMAGE_ALT}
          caption="What is a technology business incubator? Founder guide"
          width={1600}
          height={1067}
        />
        </div>
        </div>

        <div data-cf-component-id={"audience-grid"} data-cf-component-type={"audience-grid"} data-cf-component-label={"Who is this guide for?"}>
          <AudienceGrid
            heading="Who is this guide for?"
            cards={[
            {
              title: 'Founders & Builders',
              description: 'For operators validating demand, pitching a vision, and moving before momentum stalls.',
              icon: <RocketLaunchIcon className="h-6 w-6" />,
              variant: 'orange',
            },
            {
              title: 'Students & Switchers',
              description: 'For readers learning how strong technical partners evaluate traction, skills, and fit.',
              icon: <AcademicCapIcon className="h-6 w-6" />,
              variant: 'purple',
            },
            {
              title: 'Community Builders',
              description: 'For connectors, mentors, and organisers helping founders meet collaborators in the right rooms.',
              icon: <UsersIcon className="h-6 w-6" />,
              variant: 'yellow',
            },
            ]}
          />
        </div>

        <div data-cf-component-id={"quote:key-insight"} data-cf-component-type={"quote"} data-cf-component-label={"Key insight"}>
          <QuoteBlock title="Key insight" variant="purple">
            {"A business incubator is a program or organisation that helps early-stage ventures develop their business. A technology business incubator focuses on startups where modern technology is the main source of innovation."}
          </QuoteBlock>
        </div>
        <div id="section-02" data-cf-component-id={"section:section-02"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"What is a technology business incubator?"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-02"}>
          <h2>{"What is a technology business incubator?"}</h2>
          <p>{"A technology business incubator, often shortened to TBI, is an organisation or program that helps startup companies and individual entrepreneurs build businesses where modern technology is the main source of innovation. It is a type of business incubator, but its focus is narrower than general business support. It is built for founders working on technology-led products, services or ventures."}</p>
          <p>{"A TBI can support founders through training, workshops, mentoring, shared facilities, business services, networking and brokering connections. Some incubators may also help with financing, investor introductions, seed funding or grant applications, though this varies by program. The common goal is to reduce early barriers and give founders a more structured path from idea to viable business."}</p>
          <p>{"The value of a technology business incubator is not just desk space. Physical space, labs, meeting rooms or internet access can help, but the stronger benefit is usually the mix of structure, expertise, community and access. For an AI or machine learning founder in Australia, that might mean finding people who understand the technical problem, meeting potential collaborators, learning how to explain the business case, and staying connected to a startup community while the idea is still forming."}</p>
          <div data-cf-component-id={"image:section-02"} data-cf-component-type={"image"} data-cf-component-label={"Image: What is a technology business incubator?"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-02"}>
          <ArticleImageBlock
            src="https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Finline-4ed7e8ee-58ec-4230-858c-834445a64383.jpg?alt=media&token=a67335ea-3b1f-4a61-a069-1d1bc244396c"
            alt="Startup notes and laptop on a shared desk at a technology business incubator workspace"
            caption="What is a technology business incubator?"
            width={1200}
            height={800}
          />
          </div>
          <ArticleCallout title="Plain-English definition" variant="brand">
            {"A technology business incubator helps technology-led founders turn an early idea into a stronger startup by giving them practical support, guidance, networks and sometimes access to funding."}
          </ArticleCallout>
        </div>
        <div id="section-03" data-cf-component-id={"section:section-03"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"How a technology incubator works from entry to graduation"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-03"}>
          <h2>{"How a technology incubator works from entry to graduation"}</h2>
          <p>{"A technology business incubator usually starts with fit. Founders research programs that match their industry, location, stage and needs. They then compare the services on offer, such as mentoring, workspace, workshops, networking and possible funding access. Many programs use an application and selection process, so founders may need to prepare a pitch deck, business plan, financial information or a clear explanation of the venture."}</p>
          <p>{"After acceptance, the incubator becomes a structured support environment rather than a one-off event. A startup may go through orientation, meet mentors, attend workshops, use shared facilities and join networking sessions. This support can last longer than an accelerator program, with some incubators working with startups for months or even years depending on the startup\u2019s needs and the incubator\u2019s policy."}</p>
          <div data-cf-component-id={"image:section-03"} data-cf-component-type={"image"} data-cf-component-label={"Image: How a technology incubator works from entry to graduation"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-03"}>
          <ArticleImageBlock
            src="https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Finline-e4a33bc3-8ca3-4391-a4bc-cbd27c1c6c92.jpg?alt=media&token=9a496986-9d2a-4287-9ce9-84ca0f65219a"
            alt="Startup incubator workspace with desks, whiteboards, and program notes in a candid campus setting"
            caption="How a technology incubator works from entry to graduation"
            width={1200}
            height={800}
          />
          </div>
          <h3>{"1. Selection and fit"}</h3>
          <p>{"The entry phase is about choosing the right incubator and making a credible case to join. Founders look for alignment with their sector, location, program length, costs and support model. A good fit matters because incubators differ in focus, from industry-specific programs to university, independent and virtual models."}</p>
          <h3>{"2. Incubation support"}</h3>
          <p>{"Once inside, the startup receives practical support to reduce early operating pressure and improve the chance of progress. This may include mentoring, training, office or lab space, meeting rooms, internet access, networking opportunities and business support services. Not every incubator offers funding, but some help founders connect with investors, grants or seed funding pathways."}</p>
          <h3>{"3. Graduation or next-step readiness"}</h3>
          <p>{"Graduation is the point where the startup is ready to operate with more independence or move to another form of support. By then, the team may have clearer goals, stronger networks, better evidence of progress and a plan for the next stage. For some companies, that next step may be fundraising, market entry, a later-stage program or continued growth outside the incubator."}</p>
        </div>

        <div data-cf-component-id={"resource-cta"} data-cf-component-type={"resource-cta"} data-cf-component-label={"Get the checklist"}>
          <ArticleResourceCTA
            eyebrow="Free guide"
            title={"Get the what is technology business incubator checklist"}
            description="Use this article as a working guide: shortlist candidates, validate traction, and structure your next conversations."
            buttonLabel="Download now"
            buttonHref="/articles"
            accent="purple"
          />
        </div>

        <div data-cf-component-id={"step-list:practical-next-steps"} data-cf-component-type={"step-list"} data-cf-component-label={"Practical next steps"}>
          <ArticleStepList
            title="Practical next steps"
            steps={[
            "What is a technology business incubator?",
            "How a technology incubator works from entry to graduation",
            "What services can founders expect inside an incubator?",
            "Types of technology business incubators and when they fit",
            "Decide whether an incubator is your next best move",
            ]}
            accent="indigo"
          />
        </div>
        <div id="section-04" data-cf-component-id={"section:section-04"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"What services can founders expect inside an incubator?"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-04"}>
          <h2>{"What services can founders expect inside an incubator?"}</h2>
          <p>{"Inside a technology business incubator, founders can usually expect a mix of practical infrastructure, advisory support and structured learning. The aim is to reduce early friction for startup teams. Instead of solving every operational problem alone, founders can use shared resources while they test ideas, build early products and prepare for market entry."}</p>
          <p>{"The exact offer varies by incubator. Some focus on physical space and facilities, while others are virtual or sector-specific. For AI and technology founders, the useful question is not only whether an incubator has desks. It is whether the program can improve access to mentors, peers, partners, customers, investors or funding pathways that match the company\u2019s stage."}</p>
          <h3>{"Infrastructure and operating support"}</h3>
          <p>{"Many incubators provide shared office space, meeting rooms, internet access and basic facilities. Some may also provide laboratories, manufacturing space or other specialised facilities where that fits the startups they support."}</p>
          <h3>{"Mentors, training and networks"}</h3>
          <p>{"Incubators commonly add mentorship, workshops and business support around the workspace. Mentors and advisors can help founders think through product strategy, business models, market entry and operating decisions. Training may cover capability gaps such as legal, accounting, marketing, finance and other business basics."}</p>
          <p>{"Networks are also a core part of the value. Incubators may connect founders with other entrepreneurs, industry professionals, possible partners, clients, investors and grant or funding programs. Not every incubator offers funding directly, so founders should check whether the program provides seed funding, loan programs, grant application help or introductions to relevant investors."}</p>
        </div>
        <div id="section-05" data-cf-component-id={"section:section-05"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"Types of technology business incubators and when they fit"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-05"}>
          <h2>{"Types of technology business incubators and when they fit"}</h2>
          <p>{"Not every technology business incubator is built for the same founder. For an AI or deep technology team, this kind of industry-specific fit can matter because the mentors, facilities, partners and investor introductions are more likely to understand the market you are entering."}</p>
          <p>{"University-affiliated incubators often suit ventures that grow from academic research, student projects or university-linked innovation. Independent incubators may support a wider mix of startups across industries. Virtual incubators can be useful for distributed teams because they provide support and resources online rather than requiring every founder to work from the same site."}</p>
          <p>{"Look at the incubator's industry focus, location, program length, mentor network, cost, equity expectations and access to funding pathways. Also check whether the program offers the kind of help you need now, such as mentoring, workshops, office space, networking opportunities, seed funding connections or grant application support."}</p>
          <div data-cf-component-id={"image:section-05"} data-cf-component-type={"image"} data-cf-component-label={"Image: Types of technology business incubators and when they fit"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-05"}>
          <ArticleImageBlock
            src="https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Finline-46ac465d-d131-4e19-beee-fa11fa636476.jpg?alt=media&token=c6a79bef-4d06-4fb7-8381-96989168b9cf"
            alt="Founder\u2019s hands reviewing AI incubator notes during a close-up mentoring session"
            caption="Types of technology business incubators and when they fit"
            width={1200}
            height={800}
          />
          </div>
        </div>
        <div id="section-06" data-cf-component-id={"section:section-06"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"Decide whether an incubator is your next best move"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-06"}>
          <h2>{"Decide whether an incubator is your next best move"}</h2>
          <p>{"A technology business incubator is a good next move when you need more than a desk. It is a program or organisation that helps early-stage ventures develop their business through structure, mentors, resources, business support and access to useful networks. Its purpose is to improve a startup\u2019s chance of survival and growth by helping founders work through early operational, financial and market challenges."}</p>
          <p>{"Before you apply, compare incubators by stage fit, technology focus, mentor quality, network strength, funding access, program length and any cost or equity terms. Common types include industry-specific, university-affiliated, independent and virtual incubators. A tech incubator usually works by selecting ventures, giving them resources and mentorship, tracking progress, and helping them move toward market readiness or graduation."}</p>
          <div data-cf-component-id={"image:section-06"} data-cf-component-type={"image"} data-cf-component-label={"Image: Decide whether an incubator is your next best move"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-06"}>
          <ArticleImageBlock
            src="https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Finline-6e291fe6-89b8-480e-8a4b-a7d2f458f709.jpg?alt=media&token=7cfcbb16-6a42-4a19-aa6c-fc5ce0fcab1e"
            alt="Startup founders meeting with mentors in a candid technology business incubator workspace"
            caption="Decide whether an incubator is your next best move"
            width={1200}
            height={800}
          />
          </div>
        </div>

        <div data-cf-component-id={"quote:keep-moving-forward"} data-cf-component-type={"quote"} data-cf-component-label={"Keep moving forward"}>
          <QuoteBlock title="Keep moving forward" variant="orange">

          </QuoteBlock>

import { useEffect } from 'react'

export const SLUG = "what-is-a-technology-business-incubator-and-how-does-it-work"

const AUTHOR_PROFILE = getAuthorProfile(DEFAULT_AUTHOR_KEY)

const AUTHOR_BIO = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.bio ?? ''
const AUTHOR_AVATAR = AUTHOR_PROFILE?.avatarUrl ?? DEFAULT_AUTHOR_AVATAR_FALLBACK_URL

interface FAQ {

}

export const faqItems: FAQ[] = [
  { id: 1, question: "How does a tech incubator work?", answer: "A tech incubator usually selects ventures, provides resources and mentorship, tracks progress, and helps founders move toward market readiness, fundraising, graduation or another suitable next step." },
  { id: 2, question: "What services can a technology business incubator provide?", answer: "Services may include shared workspace, labs, meeting rooms, internet access, mentoring, workshops, business support, networking, investor introductions, grant pathways or seed funding connections, depending on the program." },
  { id: 3, question: "Is an incubator the same as an accelerator?", answer: "No. Incubators often support earlier-stage ventures over months or years, while accelerators are commonly shorter and more intensive. Exact models vary by program." },
  { id: 4, question: "How should founders choose a technology business incubator?", answer: "Founders should compare stage fit, technology focus, location, mentor network, program length, cost, equity expectations and access to relevant customers, partners, investors or funding pathways." },

export const summaryHighlights = {

    { label: "What are the three types of incubators?", description: "Common incubator types include industry-specific, university-affiliated and independent incubators. The article also covers virtual incubators, which support distributed teams through online programs and resources." },

}

export const articleMeta = {

  category: CATEGORY,
  slug: SLUG,
  description: DESCRIPTION,
  datePublished: DATE_PUBLISHED,
  dateModified: DATE_MODIFIED,
  author: AUTHOR,
  image: HERO_IMAGE,
  imageAlt: HERO_IMAGE_ALT,
  featuredFocus: FEATURED_FOCUS,

const faqSchemaItems = [
  { question: "What is a business incubator?", answer: "A business incubator is a program or organisation that helps early-stage ventures develop their business. A technology business incubator focuses on startups where modern technology is the main source of innovation." },
  { question: "What is the purpose of a business incubator?", answer: "The purpose is to improve a startup\u2019s chance of survival and growth. Incubators do this through structure, mentors, resources, business support and access to useful networks." },
  { question: "What are the three types of incubators?", answer: "Common incubator types include industry-specific, university-affiliated and independent incubators. The article also covers virtual incubators, which support distributed teams through online programs and resources." },
  { question: "How does a tech incubator work?", answer: "A tech incubator usually selects ventures, provides resources and mentorship, tracks progress, and helps founders move toward market readiness, fundraising, graduation or another suitable next step." },
  { question: "What services can a technology business incubator provide?", answer: "Services may include shared workspace, labs, meeting rooms, internet access, mentoring, workshops, business support, networking, investor introductions, grant pathways or seed funding connections, depending on the program." },
  { question: "Is an incubator the same as an accelerator?", answer: "No. Incubators often support earlier-stage ventures over months or years, while accelerators are commonly shorter and more intensive. Exact models vary by program." },
  { question: "How should founders choose a technology business incubator?", answer: "Founders should compare stage fit, technology focus, location, mentor network, program length, cost, equity expectations and access to relevant customers, partners, investors or funding pathways." },
]

function ContentFactoryInspectorBridge() {
  useEffect(() => {
    if (typeof window === 'undefined') return
    if (!new URLSearchParams(window.location.search).has('cfInspector')) return
    const script = document.createElement('script')
    script.dataset.contentFactoryInspector = 'true'
    script.textContent = CONTENT_FACTORY_INSPECTOR_SCRIPT
    document.body.appendChild(script)
    return () => {
      script.remove()
    }
  }, [])
  return null
}

        <p><strong>{TOPIC}</strong> — {"A technology business incubator, or TBI, supports startup companies and individual entrepreneurs that use modern technology as the main source of innovation. For early-stage founders, this matters because a technical idea, research result or prototype is not yet a business. Founders still need to test the idea, shape a useful product, understand customers and build a plan for growth."}</p>
        <p>{"Incubators are designed as structured support environments, not just desks or office space. They can offer mentorship, training, shared facilities, business support, networks and, in some cases, pathways to funding."}</p>

            {

            {

              icon: <UsersIcon className="h-6 w-6" />,

            },
            ]}
          />
        </div>

        <div data-cf-component-id={"quote:key-insight"} data-cf-component-type={"quote"} data-cf-component-label={"Key insight"}>
          <QuoteBlock title="Key insight" variant="purple">
            {"A business incubator is a program or organisation that helps early-stage ventures develop their business. A technology business incubator focuses on startups where modern technology is the main source of innovation."}
          </QuoteBlock>
        </div>
        <div id="section-02" data-cf-component-id={"section:section-02"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"What is a technology business incubator?"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-02"}>
          <h2>{"What is a technology business incubator?"}</h2>
          <p>{"A technology business incubator, often shortened to TBI, is an organisation or program that helps startup companies and individual entrepreneurs build businesses where modern technology is the main source of innovation. It is a type of business incubator, but its focus is narrower than general business support. It is built for founders working on technology-led products, services or ventures."}</p>
          <p>{"A TBI can support founders through training, workshops, mentoring, shared facilities, business services, networking and brokering connections. Some incubators may also help with financing, investor introductions, seed funding or grant applications, though this varies by program. The common goal is to reduce early barriers and give founders a more structured path from idea to viable business."}</p>
          <p>{"The value of a technology business incubator is not just desk space. Physical space, labs, meeting rooms or internet access can help, but the stronger benefit is usually the mix of structure, expertise, community and access. For an AI or machine learning founder in Australia, that might mean finding people who understand the technical problem, meeting potential collaborators, learning how to explain the business case, and staying connected to a startup community while the idea is still forming."}</p>
          <div data-cf-component-id={"image:section-02"} data-cf-component-type={"image"} data-cf-component-label={"Image: What is a technology business incubator?"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-02"}>
          <ArticleImageBlock
            src="https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Finline-4ed7e8ee-58ec-4230-858c-834445a64383.jpg?alt=media&token=a67335ea-3b1f-4a61-a069-1d1bc244396c"
            alt="Startup notes and laptop on a shared desk at a technology business incubator workspace"
            caption="What is a technology business incubator?"
            width={1200}
            height={800}
          />
          </div>
          <ArticleCallout title="Plain-English definition" variant="brand">
            {"A technology business incubator helps technology-led founders turn an early idea into a stronger startup by giving them practical support, guidance, networks and sometimes access to funding."}
          </ArticleCallout>
        </div>
        <div id="section-03" data-cf-component-id={"section:section-03"} data-cf-component-type={"section"} data-cf-component-label={"How a technology incubator works from entry to graduation"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-03"}>
          <h2>{"How a technology incubator works from entry to graduation"}</h2>
          <p>{"A technology business incubator usually starts with fit. Founders research programs that match their industry, location, stage and needs. They then compare the services on offer, such as mentoring, workspace, workshops, networking and possible funding access. Many programs use an application and selection process, so founders may need to prepare a pitch deck, business plan, financial information or a clear explanation of the venture."}</p>
          <p>{"After acceptance, the incubator becomes a structured support environment rather than a one-off event. A startup may go through orientation, meet mentors, attend workshops, use shared facilities and join networking sessions. This support can last longer than an accelerator program, with some incubators working with startups for months or even years depending on the startup\u2019s needs and the incubator\u2019s policy."}</p>
          <div data-cf-component-id={"image:section-03"} data-cf-component-type={"image"} data-cf-component-label={"Image: How a technology incubator works from entry to graduation"} data-cf-source-section-id={"section-03"}>
          <ArticleImageBlock
            src="https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/mlai-main-website.firebasestorage.app/o/content-factory%2FU05QPB483K9%2FMLAI-AUS-Inc%2Fmlai-au%2Fimages%2Finline-e4a33bc3-8ca3-4391-a4bc-cbd27c1c6c92.jpg?alt=media&token=9a496986-9d2a-4287-9ce9-84ca0f65219a"
            alt="Startup incubator workspace with desks, whiteboards, and program notes in a candid campus setting"
            caption="How a technology incubator works from entry to graduation"
            width={1200}
            height={800}
          />
          </div>
          <h3>{"1. Selection and fit"}</h3>
          <p>{"The entry phase is about choosing the right incubator and making a credible case to join. Founders look for alignment with their sector, location, program length, costs and support model. A good fit matters because incubators differ in focus, from industry-specific programs to university, independent and virtual models."}</p>
          <h3>{"2. Incubation support"}</h3>
          <p>{"Once inside, the startup receives practical support to reduce early operating pressure and improve the chance of progress. This may include mentoring, training, office or lab space, meeting rooms, internet access, networking opportunities and business support services. Not every incubator offers funding, but some help founders connect with investors, grants or seed funding pathways."}</p>
          <h3>{"3. Graduation or next-step readiness"}</h3>
          <p>{"Graduation is the point where the startup is ready to operate with more independence or move to another form of support. By then, the team may have clearer goals, stronger networks, better evidence of progress and a plan for the next stage. For some companies, that next step may be fundraising, market entry, a later-stage program or continued growth outside the incubator."}</p>
        </div>

        <div data-cf-component-id={"resource-cta"} data-cf-component-type={"resource-cta"} data-cf-component-label={"Get the checklist"}>
          <ArticleResourceCTA

            "Types of technology business incubators and when they fit",
